video_title: Dambisa Moyo: Economic Growth, Governance, and Capital Allocation (Capital Allocators, EP.510)
transcript: [music] [music] Damis said, thanks so much for doing this. >> Thank you. Thank you for hosting me. >> I'd love for you to start with how you describe what you do. Now, we can go backwards from there. The best description is I sort of live at the intersection of public policy because of my house of lord seat, capital allocation from a corporate space because I sit on large global complex organizations on the boards of those companies. And then the third piece is looking and through the lens of investment through our family office um which as you know is a proceeds of the sale of Qualrix in 2018. I have uh feet in different audiences in different uh pockets but actually we're all dealing with the same things in terms of economic growth. We are all dealing with capital allocation thinking about returns thinking about headwinds everything from AI technology and the risk of a jobless underclass global demographic shifts quality and quantity of the workforce. we're thinking about natural resources. How should we be thinking about critical minerals? How should we be thinking about, you know, water, land, arable land? And then I also think that these different portals think a lot about not just the tactics where's the interest rate today you know what is non-farm non-farm payrolls number come out as but thinking about strategic changes in the global economy in geopolitics and in finance. So I don't know if that helps at all. I think I think of it less as having a title and more addressing the world's biggest shifts geopolitically, socially and economically and looking at it from different perspectives. >> Walk me through your path from early days to where the how this all came about. >> So at a high level, I was born and raised in the emerging markets. um I was born in Africa and I'm very grateful for that uh for that experience because I think it's uh it is a place still today that a lot of um there's there's clearly sort of questions around the systemic uh patterns and of society and culture that persist. But it's fascinating to me in the emerging markets where 90% of the world's population lives that a lot of the things we worry about in the west AI are going to get rid of jobs somehow can pass these societies by. And I think if I think about my own background, I feel very lucky because I didn't grow up in an ideological society. Nobody was either a Democrat or Republican or black or white or between 25 and 35 and those types of sort of allocations. We, you know, we you either were free marketeteer or you're Keynesian. I just didn't grow up in that world. Everything is very much in your face. It's a very clear reality. And uh and as as such I've now had the opportunity to travel to over 80 countries around the world, rich, poor, democratic, non-democratic, and I don't harbor or carry ideological views. Um and so that is where I would say has really driven the rest of my career because I'm I'm eternally curious. I'm genuinely interested in long-term progress, expanding growth, improvements in living standards, but I don't think after 250 years of Adam Smith, I don't think anybody has an answer. Um, and and we swing back from, you know, more Karl Marx, more Canes, more Hayak, more Smith. But when it comes down to we don't know the what creates economic growth. We we have some semblance of countries that are more successful than others. I mean, the United States is in the crucible because it's so amazing what has been accomplished in 250 years. But at the same time, I think that the reason ideologues win is that there's always some disappointment with an economic system as in the case of the US capitalist system, we've got inequities, we've got these uh um sort of externalities from climate, etc. So, I I'm I'm sure I'm just rambling on a little bit, but essentially um really the reason I'm here today is because I was fortunate enough to be born in a place where there's good reason to be skeptical of all kinds of models that are in the books. >> How did you get from that in-your-face reality growing up to being so globally aware of the different systems that exist? Well, you know, funny enough, it's because because there's so much, I would say superficially, there's a lot of things that people think are wrong in a place like Africa. They say, you know, the population is skewed young, that's supposed to be net good. Um, they have a lot of arable land. They've got critical minerals, they've got energy. Why is this place not growing at 10% a year, you know? So what happens is people come into the continent from all around the world especially the developed world with their opinions of not only an assessment of what's wrong but they come with their own solutions um and you know aid is the obvious one but even more recently the Chinese approach into Africa has not really been an aiddriven approach it's one more much more of investment both of these models have their problems but what it means is as being on continent which is a recipient of competitive ideas. there's much more openness I think to trial and error and uh it's there's a sort of perpetual tugofwar where I would argue there are a lot of costs but I think people from outside who try these things you know more China more US you know it's more democracy less democracy I think would say it's very easy to try your theories um in a place where there's uh you know relatively low growth I say relatively there's growth but it's not as where you think it might um and where the regulatory and oversight governance issues generally remain weak. Um so you can go and trial new drugs uh in a country without a lot of oversight. So etc. That's it's a simple example but that's >> and how did you find your way into exploring some of these macroeconomic issues? >> So very fortunate to have been born in the household I was born in. I grew up in a household where everything was debated, politics, economics, social trends. Um I mean we would have massive battles. Uh I grew up at a time where throwing rocks because the subsidies had been removed by the Zambian government. All very funny now because I was uh with my PhD supervisor Paul Collier not too long ago. And I remember meeting him because you know as a young student you know we were pro- subsidies. you're super left when you're 20s and then reality kicks in later. But the point just being our household was was fever pitch with ideas, competitive ideas. My parents grew up in the colonial era. Um there was a lot of communism in Africa back in the day. Glassnos and Parisa and democratic push after the 19 uh fall of the wall Berlin. And I so I was surrounded by competitive ideas in our household. Um and I was very fortunate to be able to go to school in Zambia. Uh again for the reasons I've already outlined that, you know, there's no sort of here we're going to give you drink the Kool-Aid about democracy being so wonderful and having no cost sort of thing. So >> and what were your steps to leaving Africa? >> Oddly enough was not for good reasons. Um I uh I believed I was going to be in Zambia, marry a Zambian, have children in Zambia and sort of have the you know middle class life my parents had envisaged for us having you know gone through the colonial period and then through independence. Um but we had a coup. Um the Zambian government uh it was an attempt to overthrow the Zambian government. I was a third-year student at university. I happened to be the only girl in my science class and so very fortuitously got a scholarship to come to the United States. uh came and my father had already been here did his PhD here but we'd gone back to Africa so my formative years were really in Africa primary secondary um part of university but because of this disruption of the attempted coup the university was shut down and I very fertuitously ended up coming to the United States initially to American university because it was sort of middle of the of the school year um but it was really not on my bingo card >> how did you take what you saw and evolve it into your interests and passions professionally. So I think I would like to think that my career and my experiences have actually generated more humility about the challenge and the complexity of of questions around for example economic growth and development. Where does it come from? I mean there are lots of big books and we touched on you know the Adam Smith's wealth of nations but you know there have been books David Landes there's plenty of books lots of Nobel prizes have been given for people you arguing that they've cracked the case as to why some societies grow others don't and I think that's always been an open question for me meaning I don't think I think I learned early from my background that there was no specific answer. It had to be quite tailor made and also it had to be something that wasn't sort of one and done. It had to constantly evolve because you know as we're seeing in the United States there might be some societal costs that weren't really priced in initially inequality or uh you know questions around the climate and those just weren't seen. Adam Smith didn't write the treaties that said, "Oh, by the way, if we do all this great stuff and there's a great free market, you know, with some constraints and some regulation, we're going to end up with, you know, potentially we need to think about these societal costs that come from climate change." He didn't talk about that. He didn't even see the industrial revolution. So I think um a lot of the questioning comes from actually not being raised in an ideological environment and that's definitely helped me in my corporate board work. It's helped me being on the Oxford University endowment with our family office. We've even made some mistakes. Um I would say I was the reason for those mistakes if I'm quite candid. I mean and you know I think also being at the early in the early innings of this AI super cycle also makes me really think about you know looking back on data on uh rates of returns. What where are the big wins? Where do they come from? Uh and how should I be thinking about that and not be sort of hamstrung into the into an ideological view of the world. What do you see having been around 80 different countries that the people that you're around that haven't had that experience may not see? >> I think the biggest things are assumptions about why one place works and another one doesn't. So most recently I was just in Bhutan which was a real shock for me because it's you know it's a place it's a top of the happiness index and I think people leave there with certain assumptions about how they could create economic growth in a place like this. It's got a small population you we just need to go in dump in a lot of money. I think they they discount local culture. They discount the systemic issues around how uh countries gel different aspects of government, private sector, uh not for profofits, individuals. And and by the way, it's it's as true as the difference between the US and China or the US and Bhutan as it is the US and Britain. You know, people think that Western countries are the same. They're really not. the manner in which people behave and their appetite for risk and their attitudes towards certain behaviors or policies. It's extremely different in a place like Europe. And I think the mistake people make is that they not only think they understand, they assume everybody and I understand in some respect because I do think there's a question about human nature. Are humans the same? How do they react to greed and sort of, you know, incentives and costs? But at the same time, I think, and by the way, they they think they understand the problem, but they also think they therefore understand the solution. They're like, "Well, if you just do these one, two, and three things, it's going to be fine." But I think where the mistake is is that not only are human beings extremely complex, but I think more critically, there's a lot of systemic aspects of different countries that perpetuate for long periods of time. So, Britain's a great example of that. you translate that idea of the importance of people to the various governance seats you've sat on. What are some of the lessons you've learned and some of the stories that embody those lessons from the various roles of of board work and governance? >> Yeah. So, I think the best piece of advice I got when I joined my first board about 15 years ago now was you really have to internalize that anything can happen. Like literally anything can happen. And if I think about my own board career, it's been bookended by pandemic. Um, it's had the financial crisis in there, but also a lot of very unique things that have happened to the companies. So, I had a CEO die in office at SAP Miller. We've had companies that were when I joined were trading at $60 a share go down to $7 and you really are completely vulnerable. Is this company going to go away? I had I was on the board of a company was second largest in the sector. This was SAB Miller. Everyone told us there's no way you're going to get bought by Anheiser Bush. It's impossible. It would be they have to take out the biggest bond ever in history to do this. And by the way, all the conflicts of interest, a laundry list of why it wasn't going to be possible. And guess what happened? We got bought. They did do the biggest bond. And so anything can really happen. I think also something that people breeze over is that certainly joining as a board member, you're coming in the in the middle of a movie and so your job is to very quickly figure out who's the protagonist, who's the martyr, you know, what's going on here, what's the plot, but also and this sounds like an overused word which I I tend not to really love. Um, but the cultures are very very different and I can give the examples of my own board current board experience. I, you know, I love the companies where I serve on the board, but they're incredibly different. So, Chevron is very much an engineering company. It has a lot of for for good reason, a lot of strictures, and I think the board largely reflects that. We've got a lot of engineers, a lot of technical people. Um, and that's largely a reflection of the way that the organization has worked and grown. Starbucks is a completely opposite end. I mean if star if uh if Chevron is all IQ I would say Starbucks is largely EQ and again the board is made up of people who are you know running Domino's Pizza and Nike so really understand the consumer in a much more innate level and yeah I think that that permeates into how the companies run and they they both been phenomenally successful. There's room for everyone. I'll just maybe conclude by saying one other thing which is that if you read a book like um Outsiders by uh by William Thorndikeke I'm often interested in does this apply to both a Chevron and a Starbucks or to any other company where I've served on the board Barclays Bank which has been around for over 300 years. And I I think it does. It really does. So there are similarities even, you know, notwithstanding the cultural piece that I've just alluded to. What are the similarities in being effective for you as a board member and then for the board as a whole serving the purpose of the organization? >> Yeah. So I'm asked um variance of that question a lot and I do think a lot about it. So why why am I on a board 12 seats on on average on most boards? What is it that makes a particular board specific and individual unique? and they can they can you know find 50,000 other economists in New York wouldn't have to think twice about it. I would like to think that really what these companies are looking for judg is judgment and you know notwithstanding uh Canaman's great book think fast and slow I don't know whether you can teach judgment um you kind of hope people have had enough life experience to not be too ideological which is the other piece I think most of the boards I've been on they've had enough life experiences to say well these are all a balancing act and I think when I think about the kind of candidates that I'm you know think add value to our boards. It's really people who are not ideological who show good judgment like again you know the things that happen that we just hadn't seen before the pandemic. You want to know that your colleagues actually are not ideological and have good good judgment. So I think those are the best boards. >> How about dynamics of a board that functions effectively? So this might sound trivial but I think this is actually for me it's something I found quite admirable. Um it's having the leaders on the board and by that I mean lead director for example or even chair in the case of Britain where there's a separated role not leading with their opinion. Um so I I I have one person in my May Khan who's now passed away but he was brilliant like at you know whenever there was a massive deal hiring a CEO an MA M&A transaction you know some kind of uh you know really big issue that was being affect company was being affected by he would literally go around the table and say you know Bill what's your thoughts what's your thought and I think it sounds really trivial but it's actually I'm trying to make a bigger point about making sure everybody is on record in effect and not in a negative way, but is really on record as voicing their assessment and their best judgment of of what an issue might be. Um, I think too often when people are in leadership roles, there's a risk that they lead with their opinion. Oh, I think we should do the deal now. Does anybody else have any comments? And I think those are the kind of places where uh I think companies end up with with problems. It's the group think oh if the the lead director is saying this I must fall in line. And I think giving people wide birth um I think is very very important. >> Any other dynamics or tips that you've seen? >> Well I think one of the other bits of sort of tugofwar in the boardroom has been where are the big decisions made? So if you think about risk mitigation and questions of investing as being the sort of balancing act the question is for something and I'll give an example of AI is that a full board issue to be debated or should it be sort of secounded to a committee where a committee gets into much more of a deep dive and then sends recommendations to the full board and I don't think there's a sort of settled answer on this uh and in fact I've been on boards where they had a separate risk committee uh and then other boards say no no no risk is part of audit because of controls and processes. So I think the most important thing maybe to directly answer your question is making sure that the organization and certainly the board doesn't become so rigid that it's unable to evolve itself if it needs to. Um so again we just lived through the business roundt changes in 2019 around uh you know who is it financial stakeholders or you know shareholders or broader stakeholders companies had to be willing to adapt and not just you know the the larger organizations operations but the board itself had to be able to say okay from from the board we need to think about how we're going to re-evaluate compensation or hiring or picking the CEO or you risk mitigation for the company as a whole. >> What have you seen as some of the key differences between for-profit board work and nonfor-profit board work? >> Everybody wants to be on the board of some, you know, I don't know, $4 trillion juggernaut. Sure. But I actually over time have placed a higher premium on people who have actually spent time on the board of their kids' school or a a hospital. I'm on the board of National Geographic and you know it's there are lots of different stakeholders that matter really importantly. It's not as easy as having just one bottom line. Are you making money or not? And I think that that to me is uh means it's much harder being on the board of a um of a not for-p profofit. Like have they really been in a in a situation where they're competing not just vested interest but you competing needs and having to make the decision that one is a superior need to another in a in a very constrained budget environment. I think these are all very important lessons. If you look at these different experiences you're currently having, which Chevron, Starbucks, Condast, what's the most vexing challenge that the various companies are facing that you see at the board level? >> There's a narrative that has seeped into the discourse in the United States in particular. It's quite surprising that these companies are there doing bad stuff, whatever it may be. They're bad partners. They're not supporting the community. they are on the wrong side of societal issues that corporation and and because I've you know I've been on the board of Chevron for 10 years you know Barclay's Bank has been around for 300 plus years I've been on these boards I know that they're made up and not just the boards the companies are made up of tens of thousands of people who have their heart in the right place uh and to to face a wrath at annual meetings that someow how there's like a dubious agenda that's going on I think is the biggest most vexing thing for certainly the organizations I work for because there there's you know there are big issues that need to be addressed. Energy is the obvious one. Um delivering clean, affordable, reliable energy is still you've still got about a billion to people who have no access to that around the world. It's such a key catalyst for economic growth. the fact that people don't have that and there are companies that are working to try and do that management team has to really focus on that but then also the share price goes down like those types of consequences and then maybe I mean this didn't happen to us thankfully but then you know the industry as a whole stops investing in in you know innovation because they can't afford to I mean they're knock-on effects or they get kicked out because of they don't satisfy ESG requirements and then all of a sudden the people who own the the shareholders who buying it and not only buying it at a cheap discount, but they then don't have the good best interests for society at hand, etc., etc. But I think that would be my most vexing thing for every single business I've been in. Consumer um uh you know, energy, banking, uh I I've been in mining, there's this just this increasing voice that companies are doing bad or are inherently evil is the big issue. If you look at that on the lens of say Chevron, there's the easy highlevel poke at dirty energy in a world trying to make the transition to cleaner energy. How do you think about those capital allocation decisions of generating cash flow from the assets that have been around for a long time and then trying to move the world through the company in a better direction? >> Yeah. So I think again this is where it's really first of all the sort of uh this immediate answer is we we want to be around these companies we want them to be going concerned so we're not in the business of doing stuff that's bad that's going to take us out of business that would be absurd. So taking advantage of technology for example to revisit those assets and think about how we can do it in a clean way is absolutely at the center of what we're doing. But beyond that we've done a lot we do I mean Chevron I'm not here to really speak about Chevron specifically but it's an industrywide thing. We've restructured the business models um you know when we in in the case of Chevron but it's true for other competitors. We now have a much bigger footprint that's looking at renewables the whole range of them wind solar you know geothermal nuclear etc. starting to revisit that because we want to be around and we want to be part of the solution and we have knowledge over 140 years or you know hundreds of years of knowledge of what creates energy. How do you get carbon, hydrogen, oxygen together to create energy but also start to think about new forms of energy that require investment that require questions around scaling? How do you scale solar um across across the world? Um so we're in the business of actually finding those solutions and to me a lot of the structural changes that have happened not just from the market reaction or from greater voices from uh from uh government and also uh communities but also what we have been trying to do in terms of restructuring how the company's set up operationally and how we think about allocating money is heavily reflects this idea of leaning into the future um as a very practical If you look at Starbucks over many years, one of the things that's changed are the direction of the company based on leadership. >> And would love to get your sense of taking that and how do you go about assessing people, maybe it's a money manager that you're thinking of investing with the lens of seeing how different it can be within the context of the same company. So I think the important thing that most people who've been involved in any sort of organization now you know whether it's public policy with government I mean you know we have governments the United Kingdom has had seven prime ministers in 10 years anything to do with human management um the the key important takeaway whether it's public policy uh nos or corporations around how you think about finding the people. I think what I've learned in 15 years on boards, but being in the House of Lords is trying to ask questions that are outside of uh what I say checkboxy. And you just to be clear, I wasn't at at Starbucks. I haven't been there that long. I wasn't there for all the uh the the changes in leadership. I have been on boards. What I'm very interested in is how do they calibrate for or think about navigating through things that seem implausible but could happen? If you take that lens and and put it over to your current third board of condandy Nast. >> Yeah. >> A content company with that black swan of AI.
